title: Chapter 1 — Nouns, Articles, and Adjectives
description: Singular/plural nouns, definite and indefinite articles, adjective agreement

study.overview: Chapter 1 introduces the two foundational concepts that govern nearly all of Spanish grammar: noun gender and number. Every Spanish noun is either masculine or feminine, and this gender determines which article (el, la, un, una) you use. You'll also learn how nouns become plural and how adjectives must change form to match the noun they describe.

keyTopics.title: Noun Gender
keyTopics.summary: Every Spanish noun is either masculine (el) or feminine (la). Gender is a grammatical property — not based on biology.
points: Nouns ending in -o are usually masculine: el libro, el amigo
Nouns ending in -a are usually feminine: la casa, la amiga
Many exceptions exist and must be memorized: el día, la mano
Gender affects the article and any adjective used with the noun

keyTopics.title: Definite Articles (The)
keyTopics.summary: Spanish has four forms of 'the' depending on gender and number.
points: el — masculine singular: el amigo (the friend)
la — feminine singular: la amiga (the friend, female)
los — masculine plural: los amigos (the friends)
las — feminine plural: las amigas (the friends, female)

keyTopics.title: Indefinite Articles (A / Some)
keyTopics.summary: Spanish has four forms of 'a/an' and 'some' depending on gender and number.
points: un — masculine singular: un amigo (a friend)
una — feminine singular: una amiga (a friend, female)
unos — masculine plural: unos amigos (some friends)
unas — feminine plural: unas amigas (some friends, female)

keyTopics.title: Plural Nouns
keyTopics.summary: Making a noun plural follows simple rules based on the word's ending.
points: Add -s after a vowel: el libro → los libros
Add -es after a consonant: el profesor → los profesores
Words ending in -z change to -ces: el lápiz → los lápices
The article also changes to match: el/la → los/las

keyTopics.title: Adjective Placement & Agreement
keyTopics.summary: Adjectives in Spanish usually follow the noun and must match its gender and number.
points: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine: rojo → roja
Adjectives not ending in -o stay the same for both genders: inteligente
All adjectives add -s (or -es) for plural: rojos, rojas, inteligentes
Most adjectives follow the noun: el libro rojo (the red book)

quickReview.rule: Plural: add -s after vowels, -es after consonants
Adjective agreement: match the noun's gender and number
Adjectives not ending in -o stay the same for both genders

questions.explanation: "el libro marrón" = the brown book. Marrón = brown.
